steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures on each side of the blank
steps.content: The left side is an independent clause: “The mycorrhizal association...is well documented.” The right side is a participial phrase—“sharing nutrients via fungal hyphae and the host tree's roots”—followed by a comma and then an independent clause: “the organisms form a kind of social system...” So the blank sits between an independent clause and a participial phrase that precedes another independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule for related independent structures
steps.content:
A semicolon separates an independent clause from a closely related participial phrase and independent clause when the second part elaborates on or provides evidence for the first. Here, “sharing nutrients...the organisms form” explains how the association demonstrates itself—how it is documented in practice. The semicolon marks this logical boundary while keeping the two parts tightly connected.

A comma (D) is too weak and blurs the boundary between the main assertion and its substantive elaboration. A colon (B) is more formal and typically introduces a list or quotation, not a participial phrase. No punctuation (A) creates a comma splice by leaving the structures without proper separation.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
